questions.prompt: What three things make up an ApisCP account at the configuration level? (Select all that apply.)
choices: siteinfo (identity)
A set of services with parameters (mysql, mail, ssh, etc.)
A plan (template the services inherit from)
A reseller hierarchy entry
A Cloudflare zone
A WordPress installation
questions.explanation: siteinfo + services + plan is the triad. Reseller, Cloudflare, and WordPress are optional adjuncts; an account exists and functions without any of them.

questions.prompt: What does `-c mysql,enabled=0` mean as an argument to AddDomain or EditDomain?
choices: Configure the mysql service: set the enabled parameter to false
Reset all MySQL settings for the account
Disable the mysql binary on the server
Apply a plan named mysql with version 0
questions.explanation: The -c flag follows a service,parameter=value pattern. mysql is the service; enabled is the parameter; 0 is the value (false). The same shape applies to every other service tweak.

questions.prompt: When the letsencrypt.auto_bootstrap scope is set to true, --bootstrap on AddDomain is implied.
questions.explanation: auto_bootstrap = true makes Let's Encrypt issuance happen on every account creation. --bootstrap=false is the explicit override when you don't want it for one account.

questions.prompt: Before running DeleteDomain against a customer, what's the first reflex?
choices: Take a manual backup of the account home directory
Run with --dry-run to see what would be removed
Disable the customer's panel login first
Lower the customer's quota so the delete is faster
questions.explanation: DeleteDomain is destructive and not undoable from inside ApisCP. --dry-run reports what would be removed without removing anything; always read its output before the real run.

questions.prompt: A thin custom plan named 'starter' overrides only mysql,enabled=0. An account on 'starter' is created. Where do every other service's default values come from?
choices: From a server-wide /etc/apiscp.conf file
From the .skeleton plan layered with the base plan (e.g. basic)
From the customer's prior account, if one existed
All services not in 'starter' are disabled
questions.explanation: Plans inherit from .skeleton (which contains the schema-required defaults) layered with the base plan. A thin plan only needs the values it wants to differ; the rest fall through.

questions.prompt: The MSP wants to bump disk quota to 20 GB for every account in the 'am-master' billing group. Which command is safest to run first?
choices: EditDomain --all -c diskquota,quota=20000
EditDomain --filter "billing,parent_invoice=am-master" -c diskquota,quota=20000 --dry-run
EditDomain am-master -c diskquota,quota=20000
AddDomain -c diskquota,quota=20000 --filter am-master
questions.explanation: --filter targets the right subset; --dry-run reports the impact without applying. --all is too broad. AddDomain is the wrong command (it creates accounts). Option 3 might work but doesn't show its scope before acting.

questions.prompt: A customer's WordPress site is running with Fortification MAX. The customer reports they can't install a new plugin from the WP admin UI. Why?
choices: MAX disables the WordPress admin UI entirely
MAX makes the document root read-only for the apache user, so WordPress can't write plugin files; updates need to go through webapp:update-all or a temporary Write Mode
MAX requires a paid licence and the customer is on a free tier
Plugin installs fail randomly under any Fortification level
questions.explanation: MAX is the lockdown mode where apache (the PHP user) can't write to app files. The intended workflow is platform-driven updates via webapp:update-all (which momentarily lifts restrictions), or a temporary unfortify for one-off changes.

questions.prompt: Update Assurance takes a snapshot before each Web App update and automatically rolls back if the post-update health check fails.
questions.explanation: That's exactly what Update Assurance does. It's the load-bearing safety mechanism for unattended platform-wide updates; without it, scheduled webapp:update-all would be risky.

questions.prompt: A new ApisCP account is created and DNS for its primary domain needs to be hosted at Cloudflare. What's the cleanest setup?
choices: Manually create the zone in Cloudflare after every AddDomain run
Set the platform's dns.default-provider to cloudflare with an API token; new accounts pick it up automatically
Use dns.default-provider = null and have the customer manage records themselves
Switch to the cloudflare DNS provider only when the customer asks for it
questions.explanation: Set the default provider once; new accounts inherit. Provider can be overridden per-account when a customer needs different DNS hosting, but the platform default is what drives 95% of onboardings.

questions.prompt: Which statements about SuspendDomain are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Suspended accounts can still log in to the panel by default, to read the suspension reason
Suspended accounts stop serving web, mail, FTP, and terminal
Suspended accounts have their files immediately deleted
A --reason flag can attach an explanation shown to the customer on panel login
Suspended accounts can be edited (e.g. quota changed) before reactivation
questions.explanation: Suspension is a soft-disable: panel login remains for the reason message, services are halted, files are preserved. --reason attaches an explanation. Suspended accounts can be edited; the new settings apply on reactivation.

questions.prompt: Put these steps in order for a clean Able Moose Accounting onboarding from scratch.
items: Pick the right plan (e.g. agency) and confirm its default values via opcenter:plan --diff
Run AddDomain with siteinfo,domain / admin_user / email / billing,invoice and --bootstrap --notify
Confirm the new account appears in Nexus and the welcome email landed
Ask the customer to point ablemoose.example's nameservers at the MSP's nameservers
Wait for Let's Encrypt to issue the cert once DNS propagates
questions.explanation: Plan first (decides what you're applying), AddDomain (does the work), confirm (sanity check), then customer-side DNS change, then LE issuance happens automatically when DNS resolves.

questions.prompt: Three customer accounts share billing,parent_invoice=am-master. Running `SuspendDomain am-master` does what?
choices: Suspends only the master account (the one with billing,invoice=am-master)
Suspends the master and every subordinate account that has billing,parent_invoice=am-master
Suspends only the subordinates; the master stays active
Errors because am-master is not a domain name
questions.explanation: Billing-invoice grouping is the bulk-operation lever. SuspendDomain (and DeleteDomain, ActivateDomain) accept a billing invoice and apply to every account in the group, master and subordinates alike.
